staged_record.name: ANTHEM metformin aging study
staged_record.summary: A completed phase 3 randomized ANTHEM trial testing 12 weeks of metformin or placebo in adults without chronic disease, stratified by baseline insulin sensitivity, with insulin-sensitivity, mitochondrial-function, glucose-regulation, and aging-biomarker endpoints.
staged_record.population: Adults aged 40 to 75 years, free of chronic disease, stratified by insulin-sensitive or insulin-resistant baseline status

staged_record.name: Berberine naturally aging rat AMPK muscle and cognition study
staged_record.summary: A naturally aging rat study testing six months of berberine against cognition, insulin resistance, skeletal-muscle morphology, oxidative stress, ATP, and AMPK/SIRT1/PGC-1alpha signaling.
staged_record.model_system: Naturally aging rats compared with young and aged untreated controls

staged_record.name: Berberine improved aged rat cognition, muscle markers, and AMPK-linked signaling
staged_record.summary: Berberine has an animal functional and AMPK-pathway signal but no staged human aging evidence in this pass.
staged_record.statement: In naturally aging rats, six months of berberine improved cognitive deficits, insulin resistance, skeletal-muscle morphology, serum and muscle ROS, skeletal-muscle ATP, and p-AMPK, SIRT1, and PGC-1alpha protein expression.
staged_record.population_or_model: Naturally aging rats
staged_record.time_horizon: Six months of berberine treatment
staged_record.quantitative_note: The PubMed abstract reports p<0.01 for several cognitive, insulin-resistance, muscle, ROS, ATP, and pathway-marker comparisons but does not provide a single geroscience effect size.
caveats: This is rat evidence and does not establish human aging benefit.
The source links AMPK/SIRT1/PGC-1alpha signaling to muscle effects, but the intervention is pleiotropic.
No human berberine aging-biomarker or functional trial was staged in this baseline.

staged_record.name: Berberine
staged_record.summary: Plant-derived isoquinoline alkaloid tracked as a preclinical AMPK/SIRT1/PGC-1alpha metabolic modulator for aging-relevant muscle and cognitive endpoints.
staged_record.mechanism_summary: Preclinical aging studies link berberine to AMPK/SIRT1/PGC-1alpha signaling, glucose handling, oxidative-stress markers, and mitochondrial biology, but human aging outcomes are not established.

open_questions: Whether AMPK-linked rat functional signals reproduce in larger mammalian aging models
Whether human metabolic berberine studies can be connected to validated aging-relevant biomarkers or function
How dose, formulation, and interaction risks should be handled before geroscience use

staged_record.name: MET-PREVENT metformin physical-performance frailty trial
staged_record.summary: A double-blind randomized placebo-controlled trial testing 500 mg oral metformin three times daily for four months in older adults with probable sarcopenia and physical prefrailty or frailty.
staged_record.population: Adults aged 65 years and older with slow 4-meter walk speed plus probable sarcopenia and physical prefrailty or frailty

staged_record.name: METFORAGING metformin epigenetic-age pilot trial
staged_record.summary: A 96-week double-blind randomized placebo-controlled pilot trial testing 850 mg metformin twice daily against epigenetic-age biomarkers in non-diabetic older people living with controlled HIV.
staged_record.population: Non-diabetic people living with HIV aged 50 years or older with virologic suppression and CD4+ T-cell counts above 500 cells/uL

staged_record.name: Metformin during aerobic exercise training in older adults
staged_record.summary: A double-blind randomized older-adult trial testing metformin or placebo during 12 weeks of aerobic exercise training, with insulin sensitivity, VO2 max, glucose, and skeletal-muscle mitochondrial respiration endpoints.
staged_record.population: Older adults around 62 years of age undergoing aerobic exercise training

staged_record.name: Metformin blunted some aerobic exercise adaptations in older adults
staged_record.summary: An older-adult exercise trial is an important functional and safety boundary for metformin geroscience claims.
staged_record.statement: In older adults randomized during 12 weeks of aerobic exercise training, metformin attenuated exercise-induced gains in whole-body insulin sensitivity and VO2 max and abrogated the exercise-mediated increase in skeletal-muscle mitochondrial respiration, with variable positive and negative responder patterns.
staged_record.population_or_model: Older adults undergoing aerobic exercise training
staged_record.time_horizon: 12 weeks
staged_record.quantitative_note: The PubMed abstract reports placebo n=26 and metformin n=27.
caveats: The trial tested adjunctive metformin during exercise, not metformin monotherapy.
Exercise training improved several measures regardless of treatment, so the result is a context-specific interaction.
The finding argues against broad preventive use without phenotype and co-intervention stratification.

staged_record.name: Metformin frailty-prevention trial in high-risk older adults
staged_record.summary: A ClinicalTrials.gov phase 2 randomized metformin frailty-prevention trial in community-dwelling older adults with prediabetes or impaired glucose tolerance, completed with posted registry results.
staged_record.population: Community-dwelling adults aged 65 to 90 years with prediabetes or impaired glucose tolerance and without baseline frailty

staged_record.name: Metformin
staged_record.summary: Approved biguanide diabetes drug tracked as the main clinically tested AMPK and glucose-handling geroscience candidate.
staged_record.mechanism_summary: Acts through glucose and insulin biology with indirect AMPK activation, mitochondrial complex I effects, microbiome and folate-methionine changes, inflammatory signaling, and other pleiotropic pathways; not all reported geroscience effects are AMPK-specific.

open_questions: Whether biomarker and transcriptomic changes translate into physical function, frailty, multimorbidity, or survival benefit in non-diabetic older adults
Which baseline metabolic phenotypes benefit versus worsen with chronic metformin exposure
Whether ongoing or completed larger aging-focused trials confirm a human geroscience signal without unacceptable tolerability tradeoffs

staged_record.name: MILES metformin older-adult transcriptomics trial
staged_record.summary: A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled crossover MILES trial testing six weeks each of metformin and placebo in older adults, with skeletal-muscle and adipose biopsies and mixed-meal metabolic testing.
staged_record.population: Approximately 70-year-old adults with impaired glucose tolerance

staged_record.name: AMPK and Metabolic Modulators Track Outlook
main_evidence_gaps: Metformin is clinically familiar and mechanistically broad, but direct human geroscience evidence is still mostly biomarker, transcriptomic, and short-term pathway engagement rather than durable function or multimorbidity outcomes.

Human functional evidence is not yet favorable: MET-PREVENT did not improve 4-meter walk speed in vulnerable older adults, a posted frailty-prevention registry record is mixed, and metformin blunted some exercise-induced improvements in an older-adult trial.
Baseline metabolic status appears likely to matter, especially insulin sensitivity versus insulin resistance, so a single preventive recommendation would overgeneralize the evidence.
Animal and non-human-primate support is real but heterogeneous across worms, mice, monkeys, and berberine-treated rats, with dose, microbiome, sex, tissue, and pathway-specific boundaries.
The track should not collapse metformin, berberine, acarbose-like caloric-restriction mimetics, and other metabolic modulators into one evidence claim without intervention-specific records.
strongest_current_evidence: MILES provides the main direct human biomarker anchor: short-term metformin changed glucose handling and skeletal-muscle and adipose transcriptomic pathways in older adults with impaired glucose tolerance.

Mouse and male monkey studies provide animal and non-human-primate support for metformin effects on healthspan, lifespan, multi-omics aging indicators, and brain-aging measures, with dose and translation limits.

METFORAGING is a current 96-week human epigenetic-aging pilot in older people with HIV; it was feasible and did not attribute serious adverse events to metformin, but the primary PhenoAge result was not significant.
Berberine has a staged animal AMPK/SIRT1/PGC-1alpha signal in naturally aging rats, but no human aging-biomarker or functional finding is staged here.
ANTHEM is completed and should be watched closely because it stratifies non-diabetic adults by antecedent metabolic health, but the registry has no posted results in the current record.
staged_record.interpretation_note: AMPK and metabolic modulators deserve a low-confidence human_biomarker_signal baseline, driven mainly by metformin. The strongest human support is pathway and biomarker movement in small or specialized trials, while direct older-adult function and frailty results are null, mixed, or tolerability-limited. The track could become more actionable if phenotype-stratified trials such as ANTHEM show reproducible human pathway engagement with favorable functional or safety direction, but current evidence does not support a human_functional_benefit rating.
what_would_change_the_rating: A move to human_functional_benefit would require a randomized human trial showing prespecified improvement in physical function, frailty, resilience, cognition, or another aging-relevant functional endpoint, with tolerability acceptable for preventive use.

A move toward durable_disease_or_mortality_relevance would require long-term randomized evidence that a metabolic modulator delays multimorbidity, major age-related disease burden, disability, or mortality rather than only improving glucose or molecular biomarkers.

Confidence would improve if ANTHEM or similarly designed studies show that baseline insulin resistance or another measurable phenotype predicts benefit and avoids the exercise-adaptation and frailty tolerability boundaries.

The rating should weaken if completed human biomarker trials fail to reproduce pathway engagement, or if larger studies confirm functional harm, poor tolerability, B12 or renal safety issues, or negative interactions with exercise training.
Berberine or other non-metformin modulators should not inherit the metformin rating without their own human aging-relevant biomarker or functional evidence.
staged_record.earliest_plausible_window: No credible LEV-relevant window can be assigned from current evidence. By the late 2020s, completed biomarker trials may clarify whether metformin has a phenotype-stratified geroscience niche, but durable function or multimorbidity evidence would still be needed.

supporting_evidence.label: Stage assignment
supporting_evidence.conclusion: The track reaches human_biomarker_signal because metformin has direct older-adult human pathway and metabolic biomarker evidence, but it does not reach human_functional_benefit.
supporting_evidence.rationale: MILES showed glucose-handling and tissue-transcriptomic changes in older adults, while METFORAGING directly tested epigenetic-age biomarkers over 96 weeks but did not meet its primary PhenoAge endpoint. Together these justify a biomarker-level baseline with low confidence rather than a functional rating.
limitations: MILES analyzed only 14 participants with impaired glucose tolerance.
METFORAGING enrolled older people living with HIV and was underpowered for definitive epigenetic-age conclusions.
Neither study establishes broad older-adult functional, multimorbidity, or survival benefit.

supporting_evidence.label: Animal and preclinical support
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Animal and non-human-primate records support biological plausibility, but model and intervention heterogeneity keep confidence low.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Metformin has C. elegans, mouse, and male monkey signals, while berberine has an aged-rat AMPK/SIRT1/PGC-1alpha functional signal. These findings show metabolic-modulator tractability across models but do not create direct human aging efficacy.
limitations: The worm effect is microbiome and glucose-context dependent.
The mouse metformin result is dose-sensitive, with high-dose toxicity.
The monkey study is male-only and biomarker-heavy rather than lifespan evidence.
Berberine aging evidence staged here is animal-only.

supporting_evidence.label: Functional boundary
supporting_evidence.conclusion: The track should not be upgraded to human_functional_benefit because direct older-adult functional evidence is null, mixed, or tolerability-limited.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Metformin attenuated some exercise-induced improvements in older adults, MET-PREVENT did not improve 4-meter walk speed and reported poor tolerability, and NCT02570672 registry results do not establish clear frailty-prevention benefit.
limitations: The exercise trial tested metformin with aerobic training rather than monotherapy.
MET-PREVENT was small and enrolled physically vulnerable older adults.
The NCT02570672 functional interpretation relies on registry-posted outcome tables rather than a peer-reviewed paper.

supporting_evidence.label: Momentum
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Momentum is mixed because clinically relevant trials exist and continue to report, but recent functional and epigenetic-aging data do not yet clearly strengthen the rating.
supporting_evidence.rationale: METFORAGING was published in 2026, ANTHEM is completed but lacks posted results, and direct frailty or physical-performance evidence has not shown a clean benefit. The track remains active, but recent clinical signals are not uniformly positive.
limitations: A completed registry without posted results cannot support benefit or harm.
Published recent human data remain small or population-specific.
Larger, phenotype-stratified outcome data are still missing.

supporting_evidence.label: Confidence and safety
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Confidence remains low because dose, phenotype, co-intervention, and tolerability boundaries are material.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The staged evidence includes high-dose mouse toxicity, variable exercise-trial response, MET-PREVENT tolerability concerns, registry GI adverse events, and a plausible dependence on antecedent metabolic health that ANTHEM was designed to test.
limitations: Preventive geroscience use requires a higher tolerability bar than disease treatment.
Response may differ in insulin-sensitive versus insulin-resistant people.
No staged trial has yet shown that biomarker movement produces durable clinical benefit.

staged_record.name: Autophagy Induction Track Outlook
main_evidence_gaps: The best-controlled 12-month spermidine trial in older adults was null on its primary memory endpoint and secondary outcomes.

The new spermidine vaccine-response signal is a small pilot result concentrated in baseline vaccine non-responders, not proof of clinical infection prevention, frailty improvement, mortality relevance, or broad healthy-aging benefit.
Most positive human signals are still short-term biomarker, immune-response, or metabolic shifts rather than long-lasting evidence of better aging-relevant function.
The track overlaps adjacent nutrient-sensing, immune-rejuvenation, and metabolic interventions, which makes clean attribution to autophagy difficult.
strongest_current_evidence: A 2026 spermidine pilot in older vaccine non-responders linked autophagy-related B-cell signals with improved vaccine-response markers, while remaining small and exploratory.
Short fasting-mimicking diet trials report human changes in autophagic flux markers alongside metabolic movement.
Spermidine interventions appear tolerable in older adults and keep the track clinically visible even though efficacy remains mixed.
staged_record.interpretation_note: Autophagy induction has a stronger human signal after a small spermidine vaccine-response pilot in older adults, but the public read should stay tentative. The new result links autophagy-related immune-cell markers to vaccine-response improvement in a non-responder subgroup; it does not yet show broad, repeated, or long-lasting healthy-lifespan benefit.
what_would_change_the_rating: A stronger stage assignment would need repeated human studies where measured autophagy activity tracks with long-lasting functional, disease, or mortality-relevant outcomes.

A firmer read would need larger and longer trials with independent replication, clearer endpoint relevance, and better attribution to macroautophagic flux rather than broad nutrient-sensing or immune-modulation effects.
The rating would weaken if longer-duration human trials repeatedly remain null or if safety, tolerability, or target-measurement problems become more visible.

supporting_evidence.label: Stage assignment
supporting_evidence.conclusion: The track remains at human biomarker signal because human studies show autophagy-marker, immune-cell, and metabolic-marker movement, but not repeated long-lasting aging-relevant improvement in human function.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Fasting-mimicking diet studies directly measured autophagy-related molecular outcomes, and the 2026 spermidine pilot links immune-cell autophagy signals to vaccine-response markers. The new signal is still too small and subgroup-limited to move the track to broad long-lasting improvement in human function.
limitations: The FMD studies are short-term.
The spermidine vaccine pilot was small and concentrated its strongest effect in baseline vaccine non-responders.
The endpoints are target, immune-response, and metabolic markers rather than long-lasting healthy-lifespan outcomes.

supporting_evidence.label: Main blocker
supporting_evidence.conclusion: The strongest longer-term spermidine trial still prevents a stronger public read, and the new vaccine-response pilot does not remove the need for larger replicated functional evidence.
supporting_evidence.rationale: A 12-month randomized trial is more duration-relevant than the short biomarker studies, and its null primary memory result keeps the track mixed. The new pilot is positive but small, subgroup-limited, and vaccine-response-specific.
limitations: The null SmartAge result is a spermidine result, not a decisive test of every autophagy-inducing intervention.
The vaccine-response signal is clinically legible but does not show infection outcomes, frailty, lifespan, or broad healthy-aging benefit.

supporting_evidence.label: Momentum
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Momentum remains mixed: a new older-adult spermidine immune-response signal adds movement, while null longer-duration data and heterogeneous interventions prevent a stronger read.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The evidence set now contains visible human activity, target-linked immune-cell biology, and vaccine-response movement, but it has not converted into repeated evidence of long-lasting functional improvement.
limitations: Recent activity is mostly biomarker movement, immune-response markers, and short-term safety work.
The evidence comes from heterogeneous interventions rather than one coherent program.

supporting_evidence.label: Read firmness
supporting_evidence.conclusion: The read remains tentative because the positive evidence is short-term, biomarker-weighted, or subgroup-limited, while the most duration-relevant efficacy trial was null.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The cited studies are useful enough to maintain a public baseline and add the new spermidine signal, but not strong enough to support a firm claim about long-lasting healthy-lifespan benefit.
limitations: No cited source demonstrates long-lasting disease, mortality, or broad healthy-aging relevance.
Safety and evidence that intended targets changed do not establish broad efficacy.
The new vaccine-response result needs replication in a larger, prospectively balanced trial.

supporting_evidence.label: Best current signal
supporting_evidence.conclusion: The 2026 spermidine vaccine-response pilot and short fasting-mimicking diet studies provide the clearest current human signals that autophagy-related targets moved.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The spermidine pilot connected immune-cell autophagy and senescence markers with vaccine-response improvement in older non-responders, while FMD studies directly measured autophagy-related molecular outcomes alongside metabolic changes.
limitations: The spermidine result is a pilot subgroup signal.
The FMD studies do not isolate whether autophagy itself is the causal lever versus broader nutrient-sensing and metabolic effects.

supporting_evidence.label: Safety and clinical visibility
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Spermidine remains clinically visible because small human trials suggest tolerability, but efficacy remains unresolved.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The safety/tolerability findings justify continued watchfulness without counting as proof of long-lasting benefit.
limitations: The trials were small and short.
The high-purity spermidine study saw minimal circulating polyamine movement, so target change is not settled.
The 2026 vaccine-response pilot used a conservative 6 mg/day dose and was not designed as a broad efficacy trial.

staged_record.name: Autophagy Induction Track Outlook
main_evidence_gaps: The best-controlled 12-month spermidine trial in older adults was null on its primary memory endpoint and secondary outcomes.
Most positive human signals are short-term biomarker or metabolic shifts rather than durable functional aging benefits.
The track overlaps adjacent nutrient-sensing and metabolic interventions, which makes clean attribution to autophagy difficult.
strongest_current_evidence: Short fasting-mimicking diet trials report human changes in autophagic flux markers alongside metabolic movement.
Spermidine interventions appear tolerable in older adults and keep the track clinically visible even though efficacy remains mixed.
staged_record.interpretation_note: Autophagy induction now deserves a public track baseline because human interventions can point to pathway-level and metabolic movement. But the current evidence remains mixed, short-term, and far from demonstrating durable healthy-lifespan benefit.

staged_record.name: Acarbose extended HET3 mouse lifespan with stronger male effects
staged_record.summary: A repeated ITP acarbose study provides robust animal lifespan support with an important sex-dependent boundary.
staged_record.statement: In genetically heterogeneous mice tested across three sites, dietary acarbose significantly changed lifespan at 400, 1000, and 2500 ppm, with 16-17% median-longevity increases in males and 4-5% increases in females at the two higher doses.
staged_record.population_or_model: Genetically heterogeneous UM-HET3 mice
staged_record.time_horizon: Long-term dietary treatment across lifespan
staged_record.quantitative_note: Age at the 90th percentile increased 8-11% in males at each dose and 3% in females only at 1000 ppm.
caveats: This is animal lifespan evidence, not human aging evidence.
The effect was materially larger in male mice than female mice.
Acarbose is approved for diabetes, but this study does not establish preventive geroscience value in humans.

staged_record.name: Acarbose HET3 mouse lifespan dose study
staged_record.summary: A three-site NIA Interventions Testing Program mouse study testing 400, 1000, and 2500 ppm dietary acarbose.
staged_record.model_system: Genetically heterogeneous UM-HET3 mice at three ITP sites

staged_record.name: Acarbose
staged_record.summary: Alpha-glucosidase inhibitor tracked as a glucose-spike blunting caloric-restriction-mimetic candidate with robust mouse lifespan and healthspan signals but no direct human aging trial evidence.
staged_record.mechanism_summary: Blunts postprandial carbohydrate digestion and glucose excursions, creating a glucose-handling intervention axis adjacent to caloric restriction and nutrient-sensing biology.

open_questions: Whether mouse lifespan and physical-function effects translate to non-diabetic humans
Whether sex, baseline glycemic phenotype, microbiome state, or gastrointestinal tolerability determine preventive-use value

staged_record.name: Fasting-mimicking diet aging risk-factor randomized trial
staged_record.summary: A randomized crossover clinical trial testing three monthly five-day fasting-mimicking diet cycles against unrestricted diet monitoring in generally healthy adults.
staged_record.population: Generally healthy adults in the United States

staged_record.name: Fasting-mimicking diet biological-age and disease-risk marker analysis
staged_record.summary: A secondary and exploratory analysis of FMD clinical samples from NCT02158897 with replication context from NCT04150159.
staged_record.population: Adults participating in completed fasting-mimicking diet clinical studies

staged_record.name: FMD cycles reduced aging and disease risk markers in a randomized adult trial
staged_record.summary: The main FMD randomized trial supports human biomarker movement but does not establish durable functional aging benefit.
staged_record.statement: In a randomized trial of 100 generally healthy adults, three monthly five-day fasting-mimicking diet cycles reduced body weight, trunk and total body fat, blood pressure, and IGF-1, with no serious adverse effects reported.
staged_record.population_or_model: Generally healthy adults
staged_record.time_horizon: Three monthly five-day FMD cycles
staged_record.quantitative_note: The abstract reports 71 participants completed three FMD cycles after the crossover phase and notes stronger post hoc biomarker movement in at-risk participants.
caveats: The outcomes were risk factors and biomarkers, not functional aging, disease incidence, or mortality.
The post hoc at-risk subgroup analysis should not be treated as definitive prevention evidence.
The intervention is a specific periodic diet program rather than a simple drug-like mimetic.

staged_record.name: Caloric Restriction Mimetics Track Outlook
main_evidence_gaps: The strongest human evidence is still biomarker and risk-factor movement from fasting-mimicking diet studies, not durable human function, disease incidence, or mortality.
FMD is a periodic dietary program, so its evidence should not be generalized to drug-like caloric-restriction mimetics without intervention-specific support.
The most reproducible lifespan signal is acarbose in mice, with materially stronger male effects and no direct human aging trial.
Sponsor, intellectual-property, secondary-analysis, and short-duration boundaries remain important for the FMD biological-age claims.
strongest_current_evidence: Three monthly FMD cycles in generally healthy adults reduced aging- and disease-risk biomarkers including body fat, blood pressure, and IGF-1 without reported serious adverse effects in the main randomized trial.
A 2024 FMD analysis reported movement in insulin resistance, hepatic fat, lymphoid-to-myeloid ratio, and a median biological-age estimate, with replication context from a second completed study.
Short 2025 FMD studies already in the public record report autophagy and cardiometabolic marker movement in healthy adults.
Acarbose repeatedly extends lifespan in genetically heterogeneous mice and improves aged-mouse physical-function and cardiac/lipid measures, but this remains animal evidence.
staged_record.interpretation_note: Caloric restriction mimetics merit a first public baseline at low-confidence human_biomarker_signal. FMD supplies the direct human biomarker anchor, while acarbose supplies stronger animal lifespan and healthspan evidence. The track should not be upgraded to human_functional_benefit until a mimetic intervention shows replicated, prespecified human functional or disease-relevant benefit under tolerable preventive-use conditions.
what_would_change_the_rating: A move to human_functional_benefit would require a randomized human trial of FMD, acarbose, or another defined mimetic showing prespecified improvement in physical function, immune resilience, frailty, cognition, or another aging-relevant functional endpoint.

A move toward durable_disease_or_mortality_relevance would require long-term randomized evidence that a mimetic reduces age-related disease burden, disability, multimorbidity, or mortality rather than only changing cardiometabolic or biological-age biomarkers.

Confidence would improve if FMD biological-age and immune-metabolic signals replicate independently with transparent conflict handling, prespecified endpoints, and clear separation from weight-loss effects.

Acarbose should not inherit a human geroscience rating unless human trials in non-diabetic or risk-stratified adults show favorable pathway engagement, tolerability, and aging-relevant clinical direction.

The rating should weaken if larger FMD studies fail to reproduce biomarker movement, if biological-age effects disappear after adjustment for weight or risk status, or if tolerability/adherence problems limit preventive use.
staged_record.earliest_plausible_window: No credible LEV-relevant window can be assigned from current evidence. By the late 2020s, larger FMD or glucose-spike modulation studies could clarify human biomarker reproducibility, but durable functional or disease evidence would still be needed.

supporting_evidence.label: Stage assignment
supporting_evidence.conclusion: The track reaches human_biomarker_signal because FMD has repeated human biomarker and risk-factor signals, but it does not reach human_functional_benefit.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The 2017 randomized FMD trial reported movement in body fat, blood pressure, IGF-1, and other risk markers, while the 2024 analysis reported metabolic, hepatic, immune-ratio, and biological-age marker movement. Existing 2025 FMD records add short-term autophagy and cardiometabolic marker support.
limitations: The human evidence is biomarker- and risk-factor-weighted.
The FMD evidence does not show durable function, disease incidence, multimorbidity, or mortality benefit.
FMD is a dietary program and should not automatically generalize to small-molecule mimetics.

supporting_evidence.label: Animal lifespan and healthspan support
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Acarbose provides robust animal lifespan and healthspan support for the track, but it remains below a human stage upgrade.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The ITP acarbose study reported significant lifespan effects at multiple doses across three sites, with larger male effects, and a separate aged-mouse study reported improved physical function and cardiac/lipid aging measures.
limitations: The acarbose aging-efficacy evidence is animal-only.
The lifespan signal is stronger in male mice than female mice.
Human acarbose use for diabetes does not establish preventive aging benefit.

supporting_evidence.label: Caloric restriction context
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Direct caloric restriction supports the nutrient-sensing premise but should not inflate a mimetic rating.
supporting_evidence.rationale: CALERIE provides controlled human caloric-restriction evidence with DunedinPACE slowing but null PhenoAge and GrimAge biological-age changes. That supports the broader nutrient-sensing rationale while remaining a full dietary-restriction intervention, not a mimetic.
limitations: CALERIE is caloric restriction itself, not a caloric-restriction mimetic.
The methylation result was mixed and does not establish durable clinical benefit.

supporting_evidence.label: Momentum
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Momentum is steady because current human FMD studies and repeated acarbose animal results keep the field active, while no recent result yet changes the evidence tier.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The field has current and recent human biomarker publications for FMD, plus reproducible mouse acarbose support. However, the evidence has not crossed into replicated human function or disease outcomes.
limitations: Recent human activity is mainly biomarker movement.
Acarbose momentum comes from animal aging studies rather than human geroscience trials.
No staged result establishes durable functional or disease relevance.

supporting_evidence.label: Confidence
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Confidence remains low because the human evidence is biomarker-heavy and the strongest lifespan result is preclinical.
supporting_evidence.rationale: FMD studies support pathway and risk-marker movement, but sponsor/IP ties, exploratory analyses, short windows, and lack of durable endpoints keep confidence low. Acarbose strengthens biological plausibility but remains animal-only for aging outcomes.
limitations: Preventive geroscience use requires better tolerability, adherence, and durability evidence than short biomarker studies provide.
The biological-age signal needs independent replication and clear separation from weight-loss effects.
Mouse lifespan benefits cannot be assumed to translate to human aging outcomes.

staged_record.name: ORARIALS-01 arimoclomol ALS phase 3 trial
staged_record.summary: A multinational phase 3 randomized trial of arimoclomol in early amyotrophic lateral sclerosis.
staged_record.population: Adults with early amyotrophic lateral sclerosis

staged_record.name: Arimoclomol inclusion body myositis randomized trial
staged_record.summary: A multicenter randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of arimoclomol for inclusion body myositis.
staged_record.population: Adults with sporadic inclusion body myositis

staged_record.name: Arimoclomol NPC phase 2/3 trial
staged_record.summary: A 12-month randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of arimoclomol for neurological progression in Niemann-Pick disease type C.
staged_record.population: Children and adolescents with molecularly confirmed Niemann-Pick disease type C, many receiving background miglustat

staged_record.name: Chaperone and Heat-Shock Support Track Outlook
main_evidence_gaps: The strongest human functional signal is concentrated in Niemann-Pick disease type C, a rare pediatric neurovisceral disease, not broad aging or LEV-relevant proteostasis restoration.
The same arimoclomol heat-shock-response strategy failed to improve primary functional or survival outcomes in phase 3 ALS and randomized inclusion body myositis trials.
Human biomarker support for generic heat-shock or chaperone induction is thin, protocol-dependent, and not clearly tied to durable tissue proteostasis.
Intervention mechanisms are heterogeneous: arimoclomol co-induction, BGP-15 metabolic effects, heat exposure, and genetic HSF-1 biology should not be collapsed into one efficacy claim.
Safety and tolerability remain material for drug programs, including hypersensitivity warnings for Miplyffa and higher discontinuation or adverse-event burdens in some arimoclomol trials.
strongest_current_evidence: Arimoclomol slowed NPC disease progression in a randomized phase 2/3 trial and was approved by FDA as Miplyffa with miglustat for NPC neurological symptoms.
HSF-1 and small heat-shock-protein biology connects proteostasis to lifespan and aggregation phenotypes in C. elegans.
BGP-15 supplies limited human proof-of-concept biomarker context for an HSP co-inducer, though the measured endpoint was insulin sensitivity rather than proteostasis.
staged_record.interpretation_note: Chaperone and heat-shock support deserves a first public baseline at human_functional_benefit because one HSP co-inducer now has randomized functional evidence and regulatory approval in NPC. The baseline must stay low confidence with mixed momentum: the signal is rare-disease-specific, arimoclomol failed in ALS and inclusion body myositis, and generic heat-shock biomarker evidence remains too thin for a broad aging claim.
what_would_change_the_rating: A stronger stage assignment would need replicated human functional benefit in age-relevant or proteostasis-relevant populations with explicit chaperone or heat-shock pathway engagement.
Higher confidence would need independent studies separating disease-specific arimoclomol benefit from general proteostasis support and showing tolerable long-term use.
The rating would weaken if additional human arimoclomol or HSP co-inducer programs fail functional endpoints, or if safety and tolerability signals outweigh narrow benefits.
Generic heat-exposure claims should not influence the rating unless repeated protocols show reproducible pathway engagement and functional or disease-relevant outcomes in humans.
staged_record.earliest_plausible_window: No credible LEV-relevant window can be assigned before replicated human functional benefit exists outside rare NPC and before pathway engagement is measured in aging-relevant tissues.

supporting_evidence.label: Stage assignment
supporting_evidence.conclusion: The track reaches human functional benefit because arimoclomol has randomized NPC functional evidence and FDA approval in a disease-specific neurological indication.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The NPC trial measured clinical severity progression, the post-hoc 4-domain analysis supports the approval-era readout, and FDA approval confirms a regulatory conclusion for NPC neurological symptoms with miglustat.
limitations: The evidence is rare-disease-specific and mostly pediatric.
The approval is for NPC neurological symptoms with miglustat, not aging or broad proteostasis restoration.
The post-hoc rescored analysis is not an independent trial.

supporting_evidence.label: Disease-general boundary
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Momentum is mixed because arimoclomol failed major non-NPC disease programs despite plausible proteostasis rationale.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The ALS phase 3 trial was null for function and survival, and the inclusion body myositis trial was null for IBMFRS function. These results prevent extrapolation from NPC to broad neurodegenerative or protein-aggregation indications.
limitations: ALS and inclusion body myositis are disease-specific contexts and do not test every chaperone-support intervention.
They are still large enough boundary conditions to constrain broad claims about arimoclomol.

supporting_evidence.label: Mechanistic and biomarker context
supporting_evidence.conclusion: The track has durable biological plausibility, but human biomarker evidence outside NPC remains indirect or inconsistent.
supporting_evidence.rationale: HSF-1 regulates lifespan and aggregation phenotypes in C. elegans, BGP-15 improved insulin-sensitivity biomarkers in a short human trial, and a small hyperthermia study did not show significant PBMC HSP70 induction.
limitations: Worm lifespan biology is not a human intervention result.
BGP-15 measured metabolic biomarkers rather than direct proteostasis outcomes.
The hyperthermia biomarker study was small but cautions against assuming easy HSP70 induction in humans.

supporting_evidence.label: Confidence
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Confidence remains low because the evidence base is heterogeneous and not yet aligned with broad aging-relevant endpoints.
supporting_evidence.rationale: A rare-disease approval raises the stage, but null disease programs, indirect biomarkers, and limited pathway-engagement data prevent a confident claim that chaperone or heat-shock support can alter aging trajectories.
limitations: No cited human study demonstrates LEV-relevant disease prevention, lifespan, or broad healthy-aging benefit.
Interventions and mechanisms in the evidence set are not interchangeable.
Long-term safety for chronic chaperone or heat-shock modulation in aging populations is unresolved.

staged_record.name: Butyrate recovered histone acylation and extended mitochondrial-deficiency mouse lifespan
staged_record.summary: A 2026 disease-model result adds recent mammalian context but is not a normal-aging or human trial signal.
staged_record.statement: In inducible TFAM-deficient mice, tributyrin/butyrate supplementation delayed multiple signs of multimorbidity, extended lifespan, and recovered intestinal epigenetic histone acylation marks lost with mitochondrial deficiency.
staged_record.population_or_model: Inducible TFAM-deficient mice with premature mitochondrial-dysfunction multimorbidity
staged_record.time_horizon: Post-induction disease course and survival follow-up
staged_record.quantitative_note: The PubMed abstract reports lifespan extension but does not provide a percentage effect size.
caveats: This is a severe mitochondrial-deficiency disease model, not normal aging.
The intervention is also microbiome/metabolite and mitochondrial-context biology, not isolated chromatin modulation.
No human mitochondrial-disease or aging trial result is established here.

staged_record.name: Chromatin and Histone Modulation Track Outlook
main_evidence_gaps: There is no direct human aging trial showing that a chromatin- or histone-targeted intervention improves aging-relevant biomarkers, function, disease progression, or survival.
The strongest mammalian signals are genetic or transgenic: Kat7 inactivation and SIRT6 overexpression extend mouse lifespan but are not yet drug-like clinical interventions.

Small-molecule signals are mechanism-diverse, including SIRT1 activation, spermidine-linked acetyltransferase/autophagy biology, beta-hydroxybutyrate HDAC biology, and butyrate-linked histone acylation in mitochondrial-deficiency mice.

Several positive findings sit at track boundaries with autophagy, nutrient sensing, mitochondrial dysfunction, dysbiosis, senescence, or DNA repair, so the chromatin contribution is not always isolated.
Histone acetylation and methylation are developmentally and tissue-context sensitive, leaving unresolved safety and tissue-specificity questions for chronic modulation.
strongest_current_evidence: KAT7 inactivation reduced H3K14 acetylation and senescence markers in progeroid human cells and extended lifespan in aged and progeroid mice.
SIRT6 overexpression extended lifespan in two mouse studies, including a later B6 study reporting reduced frailty and lifespan extension in both sexes.
SRT1720 supplementation improved mouse healthspan and increased mean lifespan on a standard diet, although maximum-lifespan and median-lifespan boundaries remain important.
Spermidine and beta-hydroxybutyrate connect acetylation biology to model-organism longevity, but their effects overlap strongly with autophagy and metabolic signaling.
H3K4me3 and H3K27me3 modifier studies in C. elegans show that histone methylation state can regulate organismal lifespan.

A 2026 butyrate/tributyrin study adds mammalian disease-model support by linking restored intestinal histone acylation marks to delayed multimorbidity and lifespan extension in mitochondrial-deficiency mice.
staged_record.interpretation_note: Chromatin and histone modulation has enough direct animal lifespan and healthspan evidence for a low-confidence animal_signal baseline. The best support comes from KAT7 and SIRT6 mouse work plus repeated C. elegans histone-modifier signals. The track should not be upgraded to human_biomarker_signal because human evidence is currently cell-based, indirect, or adjacent to other tracks, and no human program has shown controlled chromatin pathway engagement tied to aging-relevant outcomes.
what_would_change_the_rating: A move to human_biomarker_signal would require a human intervention study showing reproducible chromatin or histone pathway engagement in aging-relevant tissue, with prespecified aging-relevant biomarkers and acceptable short-term safety.
A move to human_functional_benefit would require a human trial linking a defined chromatin/histone intervention to functional benefit in older adults or a clearly aging-relevant disease population.
Confidence would improve if mammalian studies replicate KAT7, SIRT6, or selective histone-modifier benefits with durable tissue-specific safety, dose-response, and mechanistic rescue experiments.
The rating would weaken if mammalian studies show unacceptable cancer, developmental, fertility, immune, neurocognitive, or tissue-repair tradeoffs from chronic chromatin modulation.

Boundary-track interventions should not upgrade this track unless they measure chromatin or histone engagement directly and show that the benefit depends on that mechanism rather than broader autophagy, NAD, nutrient-sensing, microbiome, or mitochondrial effects.
staged_record.earliest_plausible_window: No credible LEV-relevant window can be assigned before selective chromatin or histone modulators show human pathway engagement, durable safety, and aging-relevant functional or disease-modifying outcomes.

supporting_evidence.label: Stage assignment
supporting_evidence.conclusion: The track reaches animal_signal because multiple animal studies connect chromatin or histone modulation to lifespan, healthspan, senescence, or multimorbidity outcomes.
supporting_evidence.rationale: KAT7 inactivation, SIRT6 overexpression, SRT1720 supplementation, spermidine-linked acetylation/autophagy biology, beta-hydroxybutyrate HDAC biology, H3K4/H3K27 modifier genetics, and recent butyrate histone-acylation work all support organismal aging relevance outside cell culture.
limitations: The evidence map mixes mouse, worm, fly, yeast, and human-cell evidence.
Several interventions are genetic or transgenic rather than deployable drugs.
Some mechanisms are pleiotropic and overlap other tracks.

supporting_evidence.label: Human translation boundary
supporting_evidence.conclusion: The track should not be upgraded to human_biomarker_signal because no cited human intervention study directly tests chromatin or histone modulation against aging-relevant outcomes.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The human material in this baseline is cell-level or adjacent: KAT7 was identified in progeroid human mesenchymal precursor cells and spermidine improved survival of human immune cells, but the outcome-driving lifespan and healthspan evidence remains animal or model-organism evidence.
limitations: Human cell data do not establish organismal safety, dosing, or benefit.
No clinical program in the evidence map demonstrates chromatin pathway engagement in aging-relevant tissue.
Adjacent human spermidine cognition data in the repository do not establish chromatin-mediated benefit.

supporting_evidence.label: Mammalian signal
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Mammalian evidence is meaningful but still preclinical and mechanism-diverse.
supporting_evidence.rationale: KAT7 inactivation and SIRT6 overexpression give the strongest lifespan evidence, SRT1720 adds a small-molecule sirtuin-activation healthspan/lifespan signal, and 2026 tributyrin/butyrate work links histone acylation restoration to lifespan extension in a severe mitochondrial-deficiency model.
limitations: Two of the strongest signals rely on genetic overexpression or gene editing.
SRT1720 is broad SIRT1/NAD biology and did not increase 90th percentile survival in the standard-diet arm.
The butyrate result is a mitochondrial-deficiency disease model rather than normal aging.

supporting_evidence.label: Mechanistic breadth and confidence
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Confidence remains low because the evidence is broad enough to justify a baseline but not coherent enough to define a safe, selective, human-ready intervention class.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The support spans histone acetyltransferase suppression, sirtuin deacetylation, small-molecule SIRT1 activation, histone deacetylase inhibition, H3K4 and H3K27 methylation genetics, autophagy induction, microbiome-derived butyrate, and mitochondrial disease rescue.
limitations: Chromatin marks control many developmental and cell-state programs.
The same mechanisms can plausibly affect cancer risk, immune state, fertility, or tissue repair.
Boundary-track overlap makes attribution difficult without direct rescue or pathway-engagement experiments.

supporting_evidence.label: Momentum
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Momentum is steady because foundational chromatin-aging biology is strong and a 2026 mouse disease-model result adds current support, but translation remains early.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The track has repeated model-organism and mammalian signals across more than a decade, plus new butyrate/histone-acylation evidence. The limiting factor is not plausibility; it is selective human translation.
limitations: Recent progress is not yet clinical.
The newest mammalian lifespan result is disease-model and microbiome/metabolite-linked.
The track still lacks normalized intervention records for most candidate mechanisms.

staged_record.name: Tributyrin mitochondrial-deficiency mouse lifespan study
staged_record.summary: Mouse experiments testing microbiota transfer and tributyrin/butyrate supplementation in inducible TFAM-deficient mice with premature multimorbidity.
staged_record.model_system: Inducible TFAM-deficient mice and mtDNA-mutator mouse context for mitochondrial dysfunction, dysbiosis, butyrate depletion, histone acylation, multimorbidity, and lifespan

staged_record.name: Aged HSCs were refractory to bloodborne rejuvenation
staged_record.summary: Aged hematopoietic stem cells did not regain youthful function after bloodborne systemic rejuvenation interventions in mice.
staged_record.statement: Aged hematopoietic stem cells remained functionally refractory after bloodborne systemic rejuvenation interventions, showing that circulating-factor modulation is not uniformly rejuvenating across aged stem-cell compartments.
staged_record.population_or_model: Aged mouse hematopoietic stem cells and functional assays
staged_record.time_horizon: Post-intervention hematopoietic stem cell assessment
staged_record.quantitative_note: This finding is staged as boundary evidence against broad tissue-general claims.
caveats: The result is animal evidence and tissue-compartment specific.
It does not rule out effects in other tissues or with more targeted factors.
It does limit claims that young systemic environments automatically rejuvenate all aged cell types.

staged_record.name: AMBAR plasma exchange Alzheimer disease trial
staged_record.summary: A randomized phase 2b/3 trial of repeated plasma exchange with albumin replacement, with or without intravenous immunoglobulin, for mild-to-moderate Alzheimer disease.
staged_record.population: People with mild-to-moderate Alzheimer disease

staged_record.name: Heterochronic parabiosis aged progenitor rejuvenation study
staged_record.summary: Aged mice were surgically joined to young mice to test whether exposure to a young systemic environment could restore aged tissue progenitor responses.
staged_record.model_system: Aged mice in heterochronic parabiotic pairings with young mice

staged_record.name: Aged HSC refractory bloodborne rejuvenation study
staged_record.summary: A mouse study testing whether aged hematopoietic stem cells recover youthful function after bloodborne systemic rejuvenation interventions.
staged_record.model_system: Aged mice and hematopoietic stem cell functional assays after systemic rejuvenation interventions

staged_record.name: Neutral blood exchange and plasma dilution rejuvenation study
staged_record.summary: Aged mice underwent neutral blood exchange with saline-albumin replacement, with human therapeutic plasma exchange serum context included for translational comparison.
staged_record.model_system: Aged mice receiving plasma dilution through neutral blood exchange, with human TPE serum analyses

staged_record.name: Plasma dilution improved aged mouse tissue readouts
staged_record.summary: Neutral blood exchange with saline-albumin replacement improved multiple aged mouse tissue readouts and motivated plasma-dilution rather than young-factor replacement models.
staged_record.statement: Diluting old plasma with saline-albumin replacement improved aged mouse readouts across muscle, liver, and brain-related assays, suggesting that reducing pro-aging plasma factors can be sufficient for some rejuvenation phenotypes.
staged_record.population_or_model: Aged mice undergoing neutral blood exchange, with human TPE serum context
staged_record.time_horizon: Post-exchange tissue and serum-response assays
staged_record.quantitative_note: The baseline treats the strongest efficacy evidence as animal-level despite the translational human serum context.
caveats: The direct intervention evidence is primarily in mice.
Human therapeutic plasma exchange context in this source does not establish human functional benefit.
Plasma dilution may act through broad factor removal rather than a defined target.

staged_record.name: PLASMA young plasma Alzheimer disease trial
staged_record.summary: A small young plasma infusion study in mild-to-moderate Alzheimer disease with randomized crossover and open-label components.
staged_record.population: People with mild-to-moderate Alzheimer disease

staged_record.name: Randomized TPE multi-omics healthy-adult trial
staged_record.summary: A randomized placebo-controlled trial comparing TPE, TPE plus IVIG, and sham in adults over 50 with multi-omic aging-marker readouts.
staged_record.population: Adults older than 50 years

staged_record.name: Circulating Factor Modulation Track Outlook
main_evidence_gaps: The strongest human functional signal is Alzheimer disease-specific plasma exchange with albumin replacement, not broad healthy-aging or LEV-relevant benefit.
Healthy-adult TPE evidence is currently biomarker-level, with small samples and surrogate biological-age or multi-omic endpoints.
Animal evidence is strong for systemic-milieu effects but tissue specific; aged hematopoietic stem cells can remain refractory to bloodborne rejuvenation.
Interventions are heterogeneous and invasive: parabiosis, plasma dilution, young plasma, TPE, albumin replacement, IVIG, and candidate proteins should not be collapsed into one efficacy claim.
Safety, durability, donor-product variability, and mechanism attribution remain central limits for chronic or preventive use.
strongest_current_evidence: AMBAR reported less functional and global decline in mild-to-moderate Alzheimer disease after repeated plasma exchange with albumin replacement.
A randomized placebo-controlled TPE trial in adults older than 50 reported favorable multi-omic and biological-age biomarker movement, strongest with TPE plus IVIG.
Heterochronic parabiosis, plasma dilution, and human cord plasma studies show that systemic blood or plasma signals can alter aged mouse tissue function.
staged_record.interpretation_note: Circulating factor modulation has enough human evidence for a first public baseline at human_functional_benefit, but only with a narrow boundary: the functional signal is disease-specific Alzheimer plasma exchange, while the healthier-adult aging signal is still biomarker-level. The track should remain low confidence with mixed momentum until replicated human studies show durable functional benefit, defined active components, and acceptable safety in aging-relevant populations.
what_would_change_the_rating: Higher confidence would require independent replication of plasma-exchange or plasma-factor interventions with prespecified functional, intrinsic-capacity, disease, or durability endpoints in older adults.
A stronger broad-aging interpretation would need mechanisms that separate factor removal, albumin replacement, IVIG, donor plasma content, and procedure effects.
The rating would weaken if sham-controlled TPE biomarker findings fail replication, if AMBAR-like functional signals do not reproduce, or if safety and feasibility burdens outweigh limited benefit.
Single-factor candidates should not upgrade the track unless they show target engagement, reproducible tissue effects, and safety in human studies.
staged_record.earliest_plausible_window: No credible LEV-relevant window can be assigned before replicated human functional benefit exists outside narrow disease contexts and before long-term safety is clearer.

supporting_evidence.label: Stage assignment
supporting_evidence.conclusion: The track reaches human functional benefit because AMBAR reported functional and global Alzheimer disease benefits from a plasma-exchange program.
supporting_evidence.rationale: AMBAR is a randomized human trial of repeated plasma exchange with albumin replacement and provides a functional disease endpoint, while PLASMA supplies smaller feasibility and safety context for plasma infusion.
limitations: The functional signal is Alzheimer disease-specific.
The intervention included repeated plasma exchange, albumin replacement, and in some arms IVIG.
PLASMA was too small to establish cognitive efficacy.
Neither study demonstrates broad healthy-aging, mortality, or LEV-relevant benefit.

supporting_evidence.label: Human aging-biomarker support
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Human TPE studies now reach aging-biomarker endpoints, including a randomized multi-omic trial in adults older than 50.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The 2025 randomized trial is the strongest healthy-adult biomarker support, while the earlier small clinical study provides convergent but weaker proteomic and immune-marker context.
limitations: Biological-age and multi-omic markers are surrogate endpoints.
The strongest signal involved TPE plus IVIG, not a simple plasma dilution or young-plasma intervention.
Sample sizes are small for confident clinical translation.

supporting_evidence.label: Preclinical plausibility
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Animal studies strongly support that blood or plasma environments can modulate aged tissue phenotypes.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Heterochronic parabiosis restored aged progenitor responses, human cord plasma improved aged mouse hippocampal readouts, and neutral blood exchange/plasma dilution improved multiple aged mouse tissue assays.
limitations: Parabiosis and mouse plasma-dilution models are not clinical interventions.
The active components and tissue-specific mechanisms remain incompletely resolved.
Mouse tissue repair and cognition assays do not establish human functional benefit.

supporting_evidence.label: Boundary condition
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Bloodborne systemic rejuvenation is not a uniform tissue-general effect.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Aged hematopoietic stem cells remained refractory to bloodborne systemic rejuvenation interventions, limiting broad claims that changing circulating factors can rejuvenate all aged compartments.
limitations: The finding is animal and HSC-specific.
It does not negate other tissue effects.
It does force tissue-specific claims and prevents pan-rejuvenation interpretation.

supporting_evidence.label: Momentum and confidence
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Momentum is mixed and confidence remains low because human signals are split between disease-specific function and surrogate aging biomarkers.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The field has recent randomized biomarker evidence and an older disease-specific functional signal, but interventions are heterogeneous, invasive, and not yet tied to durable broad-aging outcomes.
limitations: No staged source demonstrates durable disease prevention, mortality benefit, or healthy-lifespan extension in humans.
Safety and feasibility burdens matter because TPE is procedural and repeated exposure may be needed.
The evidence should not be generalized to commercial young-plasma or uncharacterized plasma-product claims.

staged_record.name: Young systemic environment restored aged progenitor responses in mice
staged_record.summary: Heterochronic parabiosis moved aged mouse muscle and liver progenitor responses toward youthful repair behavior.
staged_record.statement: Exposure of aged mice to a young systemic environment restored muscle satellite-cell and liver progenitor responses after injury, supporting the idea that circulating milieu can modulate age-impaired tissue repair.
staged_record.population_or_model: Aged mice surgically paired with young mice
staged_record.time_horizon: Parabiosis exposure followed by tissue injury and repair assays
staged_record.quantitative_note: The baseline treats this as foundational animal evidence for systemic-milieu modulation, not as a deployable human intervention.
caveats: Parabiosis is a surgical animal model rather than a clinical procedure.
The study does not isolate a single actionable circulating factor.
Animal tissue repair does not establish human healthy-aging benefit.

staged_record.name: CANTOS canakinumab atherosclerosis trial
staged_record.summary: A large randomized placebo-controlled trial of IL-1beta inhibition with canakinumab in post-myocardial-infarction patients with residual inflammatory risk, with cardiovascular outcomes and later aging-relevant post hoc analyses.
staged_record.population: Patients with previous myocardial infarction and hsCRP of at least 2 mg/L

staged_record.name: Clazakizumab dialysis IL-6 phase 2b trial
staged_record.summary: A randomized phase 2b dose-finding trial of IL-6 ligand inhibition in maintenance dialysis patients with elevated hsCRP.
staged_record.population: Adults receiving maintenance dialysis with cardiovascular disease and/or diabetes and hsCRP of at least 2 mg/L

staged_record.name: COLCOT low-dose colchicine post-myocardial-infarction trial
staged_record.summary: A randomized double-blind trial testing 0.5 mg daily colchicine within 30 days after myocardial infarction.
staged_record.population: Patients recruited within 30 days after myocardial infarction

staged_record.name: Dapansutrile HFrEF NLRP3 phase 1B trial
staged_record.summary: A small randomized dose-escalation trial of oral NLRP3 inhibition in stable NYHA II-III systolic heart failure.
staged_record.population: Stable NYHA II-III systolic heart failure patients

staged_record.name: LoDoCo2 low-dose colchicine chronic coronary disease trial
staged_record.summary: A randomized double-blind trial testing 0.5 mg daily colchicine in chronic coronary disease.
staged_record.population: Patients with chronic coronary disease

staged_record.name: RESCUE ziltivekimab chronic kidney disease inflammation trial
staged_record.summary: A randomized phase 2 trial of IL-6 ligand inhibition with ziltivekimab in chronic kidney disease patients with elevated hsCRP and high cardiovascular risk.
staged_record.population: Adults with moderate to severe chronic kidney disease and hsCRP of at least 2 mg/L

staged_record.name: Cytokine and Inflammasome Modulation Track Outlook
main_evidence_gaps: The strongest human outcomes are cardiovascular secondary-prevention results in high-risk disease populations, not broad healthy-aging or LEV-relevant rejuvenation outcomes.
Cytokine and inflammasome suppression can increase infection or other safety risks, which matters for long-term preventive use.
Direct frailty evidence is not supportive: the CANTOS frailty post hoc analysis found no reduction in incident frailty.
IL-6 programs have strong biomarker movement, but large clinical outcome confirmation is still pending.
Colchicine, canakinumab, IL-6 antibodies, and NLRP3 inhibitors are mechanistically distinct and should not be merged into one efficacy claim.
strongest_current_evidence: CANTOS showed that IL-1beta inhibition can reduce recurrent cardiovascular events without lipid lowering in post-MI patients with residual inflammatory risk.
Low-dose colchicine reduced ischemic cardiovascular event composites in both recent-MI and chronic coronary disease trials.
Ziltivekimab and clazakizumab robustly lowered hsCRP and related inflammatory or thrombotic biomarkers in high-risk CKD or dialysis populations.
Dapansutrile provides an early human foothold for direct oral NLRP3 inhibition, though only as a small short safety study with exploratory functional signals.
ZEUS is a large active phase 3 ziltivekimab outcomes trial that could clarify whether IL-6 biomarker movement translates into cardiovascular event reduction.
staged_record.interpretation_note: This track earns a disease-outcome baseline because targeted anti-inflammatory approaches have reduced cardiovascular events in large randomized trials. The confidence should stay low for aging interpretation: the results are disease-specific, safety-sensitive, and not supported by direct frailty benefit. The near-term watch item is whether IL-6 inhibition converts strong biomarker effects into phase 3 clinical outcomes without unacceptable infection or laboratory safety tradeoffs.
what_would_change_the_rating: Confidence would increase if ZEUS or another large IL-6 outcomes trial reduces cardiovascular events with acceptable infection, lipid, neutrophil, platelet, and kidney safety.

The stage would become more aging-relevant if a targeted cytokine or inflammasome intervention improves frailty, physical function, immune resilience, or multimorbidity endpoints in older adults rather than only disease-specific cardiovascular endpoints.
Direct NLRP3 confidence would require randomized phase 2 or phase 3 trials with prespecified functional or clinical outcomes rather than short safety and pharmacodynamic studies.

The rating would weaken if larger IL-6 or NLRP3 trials miss outcomes, if cardiovascular benefit is offset by infection or noncardiovascular mortality, or if benefits require risk profiles too narrow for preventive geroscience use.
staged_record.earliest_plausible_window: No credible LEV-relevant window can be assigned; disease-specific cardiovascular prevention may mature sooner, but broad aging translation needs older-adult functional or multimorbidity trials.

supporting_evidence.label: Stage assignment
supporting_evidence.conclusion: The track reaches durable disease or mortality relevance because large randomized trials show cardiovascular event reduction from IL-1beta inhibition or low-dose colchicine.
supporting_evidence.rationale: CANTOS directly tested IL-1beta inhibition without lipid lowering, while LoDoCo2 and COLCOT show reproducible low-dose colchicine cardiovascular outcome benefit across chronic and recent post-MI disease settings.
limitations: The outcome evidence is cardiovascular secondary prevention, not direct biological-aging modification.
Colchicine is broad anti-inflammatory therapy rather than selective cytokine or NLRP3 blockade.
The disease-outcome stage should not be read as broad longevity efficacy.

supporting_evidence.label: Aging interpretation boundary
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Broad aging confidence remains low because the strongest targeted cytokine trial did not reduce incident frailty.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The CANTOS frailty analysis directly examined a geroscience-relevant functional outcome and found no preventive effect, even though cardiovascular outcome findings remained favorable.
limitations: The frailty analysis was post hoc and not a primary geroscience trial.
The lung-cancer signal was exploratory and hypothesis-generating.
Neither source establishes healthy-lifespan extension.

supporting_evidence.label: IL-6 translational momentum
supporting_evidence.conclusion: IL-6 inhibition has strong human biomarker momentum, with phase 3 outcome testing underway.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Ziltivekimab and clazakizumab strongly reduced hsCRP and related inflammatory biomarkers in CKD or dialysis populations, and ZEUS is testing whether this mechanism reduces cardiovascular outcomes.
limitations: Biomarker reduction is not outcome evidence.
The populations are high-risk CKD or dialysis cohorts.
ZEUS results were not posted as of the registry update reviewed for this bundle.

supporting_evidence.label: Direct inflammasome signal
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Direct oral NLRP3 inhibition has early human safety support but not mature outcome evidence.
supporting_evidence.rationale: Dapansutrile was safe and well tolerated in a short phase 1B HFrEF study and showed exploratory within-cohort LVEF and exercise-time changes at the top dose.
limitations: The study was small, short, and primarily a safety study.
Placebo participants were not used for statistical efficacy comparisons.
HFrEF data do not establish general aging or chronic inflammation benefit.

supporting_evidence.label: Safety and prevention boundary
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Long-term preventive use is constrained by infection and noncardiovascular safety signals.
supporting_evidence.rationale: CANTOS reported more fatal infections with canakinumab, COLCOT reported more serious pneumonia with colchicine, LoDoCo2 had a numerically higher noncardiovascular mortality signal, and higher-dose clazakizumab had numerically more serious infections.
limitations: Safety signals differ by mechanism, population, and dose.
Some safety findings are numerical rather than definitively causal.
The boundary is still central because broad prevention would expose people for long periods.

staged_record.name: ZEUS ziltivekimab cardiovascular outcomes trial
staged_record.summary: An active phase 3 cardiovascular outcomes trial testing monthly ziltivekimab in people with atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, chronic kidney disease, and systemic inflammation.
staged_record.population: People with atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, chronic kidney disease, and hsCRP of at least 2 mg/L

staged_record.name: Thymus Regeneration, Immunorestoration, and Insulin Mitigation Extension Trial
staged_record.summary: A ClinicalTrials.gov phase 2 registry entry for TRIIM-X, listed as recruiting with no posted results as of the May 7, 2025 registry update.

staged_record.name: DNA Methylation and Epigenome Restoration Track Outlook
main_evidence_gaps: Human evidence is still DNA-methylation-clock heavy, with no durable functional, disease, or mortality outcome tied to methylation restoration.
Signals are small or inconsistent in larger controlled analyses, and stronger reversal claims come from small, uncontrolled, retrospective, or multi-component studies.
Many interventions may move methylation as a downstream readout rather than directly restoring causal epigenomic aging mechanisms.
Clock choice, tissue source, blood-cell composition, commercial tests, and conflicts of interest remain major interpretation hazards.
TRIIM-X is listed as recruiting with no posted results, so it cannot yet resolve the safety or efficacy boundary for hormone/metformin thymus-regeneration regimens.
strongest_current_evidence: CALERIE randomized caloric restriction slowed DunedinPACE over two years, although PhenoAge and GrimAge did not significantly change.
DO-HEALTH reported small omega-3-associated slowing across several DNA-methylation clocks over three years, with an additive PhenoAge signal for omega-3, vitamin D, and exercise.
COSMOS reported modest multivitamin-multimineral slowing of PCGrimAge and PCPhenoAge over two years, while cocoa extract was null.
The methylation-supportive diet and lifestyle pilot RCT reported a Horvath DNAmAge decrease versus no-intervention controls after eight weeks.
TRIIM and Rejuvant provide larger apparent reversal signals, but they are uncontrolled or retrospective and should not drive a higher stage assignment.
CASIN in aged mice links a short systemic intervention to younger blood DNA-methylation age plus lifespan and inflammatory-marker movement.
staged_record.interpretation_note: DNA methylation and epigenome restoration earns a first public baseline at human_biomarker_signal because multiple human intervention studies now report movement in DNA-methylation aging measures. The rating should stay conservative: larger randomized analyses show small and clock-specific effects, while the most dramatic reversal claims are not controlled enough to support functional or clinical benefit.
what_would_change_the_rating: A stage upgrade would require replicated randomized trials showing functional, disease, or mortality benefit with prespecified DNA-methylation endpoints that move in the expected direction.
Higher confidence would require convergent effects across multiple validated clocks, tissues, and independent cohorts, with durability after intervention withdrawal.

Mechanistic confidence would improve if studies showed that methylation changes mediate downstream health benefits rather than merely tracking diet, body composition, inflammation, or blood-cell shifts.

The rating would weaken if larger preregistered trials fail to replicate clock movement, show no relationship between clock changes and clinical endpoints, or identify meaningful safety concerns for epigenome-targeting regimens.
staged_record.earliest_plausible_window: No credible LEV-relevant window can be assigned before methylation-clock changes are tied to replicated clinical or functional benefit.

supporting_evidence.label: Stage assignment
supporting_evidence.conclusion: The track reaches human biomarker signal because several human interventions report DNA-methylation aging-measure movement.
supporting_evidence.rationale: CALERIE, DO-HEALTH, COSMOS, and the methylation-supportive diet/lifestyle RCT are human intervention studies with DNA-methylation clock endpoints. Their endpoints are biomarkers, not functional outcomes.
limitations: The endpoints are DNA-methylation algorithms rather than direct healthspan, disease, or survival outcomes.
Effects are small or clock-specific in the larger controlled studies.
Interventions are heterogeneous and should not be collapsed into one efficacy claim.

supporting_evidence.label: Controlled-evidence boundary
supporting_evidence.conclusion: The better controlled evidence supports a cautious biomarker baseline, not a functional-benefit claim.
supporting_evidence.rationale: CALERIE slowed DunedinPACE but not PhenoAge or GrimAge, DO-HEALTH showed small omega-3-centered effects, and COSMOS showed modest multivitamin effects with cocoa null.
limitations: Analyses were post hoc or ancillary relative to primary clinical trial aims.
Clock response was not uniform across interventions or algorithms.
Clinical relevance of the small clock shifts remains unresolved.

supporting_evidence.label: Reversal-claim boundary
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Large apparent reversal signals exist but are not strong enough to raise the stage above human biomarkers.
supporting_evidence.rationale: TRIIM was a nine-person uncontrolled male pilot, and Rejuvant was a retrospective uncontrolled supplement analysis using a commercial product and test.
limitations: No randomized placebo comparison exists for these reversal claims.
Both signals are vulnerable to selection, regression-to-the-mean, blood-composition, and conflict-of-interest concerns.
Neither study proves durable health benefit.

supporting_evidence.label: Animal plausibility
supporting_evidence.conclusion: CASIN gives animal biomarker and lifespan plausibility for methylation-age movement after intervention.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The aged-mouse CASIN study reported a younger blood DNA-methylation clock alongside lower inflammatory cytokines and extended lifespan.
limitations: Mouse blood methylation and lifespan data do not establish human translation.
The mechanism may be systemic or hematopoietic rather than direct epigenome restoration.
Safety and selectivity of systemic Cdc42 inhibition remain open.

supporting_evidence.label: Momentum
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Momentum is steady because larger randomized clock analyses continue to appear and TRIIM-X is registered, but posted outcome evidence is still limited.
supporting_evidence.rationale: DO-HEALTH and COSMOS are recent larger randomized analyses, while the TRIIM-X registry indicates follow-up work without posted results.
limitations: The recent randomized signals are modest and biomarker-only.
TRIIM-X is not evidence until results are posted or published.
The field still lacks replicated clinical endpoints.

supporting_evidence.label: Confidence
supporting_evidence.conclusion: Confidence remains low despite multiple human biomarker signals.
supporting_evidence.rationale: The evidence base has enough human DNA-methylation movement for a baseline, but inconsistency, small effects, surrogate endpoints, multi-component interventions, and conflicts prevent a confident forecast.
limitations: No cited human intervention demonstrates LEV-relevant clinical benefit.
The causal role of methylation-clock changes is not established.
Commercial and clock-development conflicts appear in parts of the evidence base.

staged_record.name: TRIIM reported lower epigenetic age in an uncontrolled human pilot
staged_record.summary: The TRIIM pilot is a notable human epigenetic-clock signal, but its uncontrolled, small, male-only design limits interpretation.
staged_record.statement: In nine healthy men, one year of the TRIIM thymus-regeneration regimen was associated with lower DNA-methylation epigenetic age and immunosenescence-marker movement.
staged_record.population_or_model: Healthy men aged 51 to 65 years
staged_record.time_horizon: One year of treatment plus reported six-month follow-up for GrimAge persistence
staged_record.quantitative_note: The abstract reports mean epigenetic age about 1.5 years below baseline after one year and a -2.5-year change compared with no treatment at study end.
caveats: The study was uncontrolled and included only nine men.
The regimen combined growth hormone, DHEA, metformin, vitamin D, and zinc, so the active component is unclear.
Several authors had Intervene Immune equity, roles, or related patent interests.
The result is a biomarker signal, not evidence of durable functional, disease, or mortality benefit.

staged_record.name: TRIIM human epigenetic aging pilot
staged_record.summary: An uncontrolled one-year human pilot testing a growth hormone, DHEA, metformin, vitamin D, and zinc regimen intended to regenerate the thymus and measure immune and epigenetic-aging endpoints.
staged_record.population: Healthy men aged 51 to 65 years

staged_record.name: TRIIM-X extension registry study
staged_record.summary: A randomized phase 2 registry study of a TRIIM-style combination regimen with epigenetic age, thymus regeneration, and safety endpoints.
staged_record.population: Adults aged 40 to 80 years

staged_record.name: TRIIM-X registry is active but has no posted results
staged_record.summary: The TRIIM-X registry shows follow-up clinical momentum for TRIIM-style epigenetic-aging endpoints, but it is not yet evidence of benefit or safety.
staged_record.statement: The TRIIM-X registry lists a randomized phase 2 study with epigenetic age, thymus regeneration, and safety endpoints, but ClinicalTrials.gov had no posted results in the current record.
staged_record.population_or_model: Adults aged 40 to 80 years
staged_record.time_horizon: Registry first posted May 5, 2020, last update posted May 7, 2025, estimated completion December 2025
staged_record.quantitative_note: Estimated enrollment is 85 participants, and the registry status is recruiting.
caveats: Registry status can lag real-world study status.
No results are posted in the registry.
The registry cannot support benefit, safety, or stage-upgrade claims without results.

staged_record.name: Nicotinamide mononucleotide in healthy older men
staged_record.summary: A placebo-controlled randomized trial of 250 mg/day nicotinamide mononucleotide in healthy men aged at least 65 years.
staged_record.population: Healthy older men aged at least 65 years

staged_record.name: Nicotinamide riboside in healthy middle-aged and older adults
